items.q: Why are tropical rainforest soils typically infertile?
items.correct: Heavy rainfall leaches nutrients, and most nutrients are stored in living biomass and rapidly recycled — the soil itself holds very little.
items.misconception: Because so much vegetation grows there, the soil must be fertile.
items.random_wrong: Because rainforest soils are made of clay.
items.why: The lush vegetation hides poor soil. The rainforest works as a tightly closed nutrient cycle; once cleared, the system collapses quickly because the soil cannot sustain growth.

items.q: Why does the tropical rainforest have distinct vertical layers?
items.correct: Competition for light: emergents (40m+), canopy (capturing most light), understorey (shade-tolerant) and forest floor (sparse vegetation due to low light) — each adapted to its light level.
items.misconception: Rainforest layers exist because different animals live at different heights.
items.random_wrong: Rainforest layers exist because trees grow in age order tallest to shortest.
items.why: Light availability drives the stratification; animal niches are a consequence. Students reverse cause and effect — the structure is plant-competition-driven, with fauna adapting to existing layers.

items.q: What is a climate tipping point?
items.correct: A threshold beyond which a system changes irreversibly into a new state — e.g. the Amazon flipping from rainforest to savanna, or Greenland's ice sheet committing to total melt.
items.misconception: A tipping point is just when warming reaches 1.5 degrees C.
items.random_wrong: A tipping point is when world leaders agree to act on climate change.
items.why: 1.5 degrees C is a policy target, not a single tipping point. Multiple system-specific thresholds exist, and crossing them can lock in changes that human action cannot reverse on human timescales.

items.q: Approximately how much of Amazon rainfall is recycled by the forest?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 50%. “20%” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the dominant direct cause of Amazon deforestation?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Cattle ranching. “Logging” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximately how many PgC are stored in the Amazon biomass and soils?
items.why: The real figure is 175. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
